note: These epithets refer to Rugomora Mahe, an early (c. 17th-century) king of Kyamutwara. His ascension to the throne is said to have rescued the kingdom from a long famine. The bard elaborates his epithet, "the writer-king," in another heroic ballad, King Kahigi, as "Rugomora, the writer of those who have ink" Rugomora mpandiki eyabali kaino. The ballad begins at night with the figure of a worried king, just as in the ballad King Kaitaba. The initial lines in both ballads introduce perceived threats to the throne. Rugomora's anxiety is about moral violations in his kingdom that may lead to supernatural turmoil.

The King sleeps fitfully, with his head toward Kihanja, to the southwest of his palace, and his feet toward Bujumba, to the northwest on the shore of Lake Victoria. Kihanja and Bujumba were part of the Greater Kyamtwara Kingdom that Rugomora ruled.
Mafumbo is a place in Kyamtwara near the lake. Because of the wondrous events that occur there, this ballad is sometimes entitled Mafumbo, or The Tree of Mafumbo.

Munya lizard . . . startler of warriors . . . is your heart still in your chest? This lizard proverbially inspires fear because of its resemblance to a snake. They say, Omulumwa w'enjoka yekenga omunya, The person bitten by a snake is cautious of the munya lizard.

Kimuli was the most beloved son of King Kalemera, an ancestor of Rugomora Mahe. Kimuli's murder set in motion a chain of events that led to the desolation of the kingdom and its ultimate rescue by Rugomora. Kimuli's spirit medium is said to have administered an oath of poison (see Mugasha I: 113), which may account for the name of the serpent associated with his shrine.
The lugondo spirit is the soul spirit, a vector of one's desire, embodying its orientation and strength.
These are praise epithets for Rugomora Mahe. Enkuku beans are associated with royalty.
Kikuli is a spirit whose shrine and medium was apparently on Bumbiile Island.
Mubilizi, mulinzi, and raffia are used to accompany sacrifices. Here, everything placed as sacrifices was gone in the morning.

The tree issues its call of defiance: The tree's antagonist may be able to stir what can't (even) be sliced: that is, he may be able to stir porridge so thick and hard that it can't even be divided into portions. But, the tree declares, the antagonist's heretofore irresistible force has met an immovable object. The tree proclaims this defiance twice - first to Rugomora (line 84.1) and then to Mugasha (line 299). As the voice of Wamara, the tree articulates opposition between the "ancient" religion of the Bachwezi spirits and the more recent royal state embodied in King Rugomora. Mugasha, the voice of non-pastoral peasants, allies himself with the kingship.
Kyandekwe Kahinda, Kakayange, and Kabogoiga are personal names. The tree boasts defiantly that it has been a wicked influence in Mahe's kingdom.
Divining to see what will kill (the tree) is a special kind of divination, oku-chweleza. The more usual divination is oku-lagula.

Entembe are coffee-bean-size seeds that grow in the fruit of a wild banana plant. They are associated with Mugasha. Coffee berries cooked by boiling are a delicacy and mild stimulant frequently offered as hospitality to guests.
Mugasha's hidden maiden, like his serving girl on line 99.1, is a reference to Lyanyaibungo, the god-king Wamara's daughter.
These images of oddity and reversal characterize the supernatural strangeness of the land to which they will travel.
Nyakalembe, Mugasha's wife, is the spirit associated with women's agricultural work. Kabagala is a kind of hoe they use in this work.
120.1 These are the bard's version of epithets for the spirit Bwogi.
These are the bard's version of epithets for the spirit Kazoba, the sun

The eight loving ones refers to the Bachwezi, a pantheon of possessing spirits. As in the ballad Kajango, the mortals first consult a succession of spirit mediums, and then meet face-to-face with a spirit who then becomes a protagonist in the narrtative.
These lines are quoted from women's wedding songs. Paddlers use them here to synchronize their effort.
The gray fowl is the thunderbolt bird that when hurled by Thunderstorm causes destruction on earth.
These epithets refer to Mugasha.

Bitoma is a village in Kihanja associated with the spirit-king Wamara and named for large Mutoma trees. These trees are frequently shrines or places of sacrifice to Wamara. In the early 20th century, Hermann Rehse recorded a belief that at death the soul of an "ordinary man" in the northern Haya kingdom of Kiziba goes to Bitoma village, where a priest of the spirit of Wamala lives.
These epithets for Wamara refer to the stereotypic lanky body and loose gait of royalty.

The hidden serving girl of Mugasha refers to Lyanyaibungo, the god-king Wamara's daughter, whose courtship by the fisher god is the subject of the ballad Mugasha. Here she is called a domestic slave, omuzana, perhaps to emphasize that she was unwillingly wed -- and then bravely rescued by her brother Kagoro. On line 104, she is referred to as a maiden, omwisiki , perhaps to emphasize her youth and beauty.
Make the plantains flee, that is, cause them to be eaten with relish.

Leg over , from Oku-kilambuza, which means literally to pass one's leg and foot over something, like a rock (to avoid tripping on it). Among children, I was told, passing a leg over someone's head is a way to start a fight.
Nsenene is a grasshopper that swarms once a year and is cooked and eaten with great relish.
Mugasha asks his minions to chant their praise (okw-ebuga) as warriors do before battle. This genre also appears in other ballads.
Tree's call of defiance: See note to  line 84.1
Americans were reported to have walked on the moon on July 20, 1969. Muzee performed Muhata a month later, and I don't think he yet believed the news reports.
